does: This paper introduces consistency training, a self-supervised approach to improve language model robustness against sycophancy and jailbreaking attacks. The core insight is to train models to produce invariant outputs across semantically irrelevant prompt variations—such as augmentations that add leading questions or jailbreak text. The authors implement two variants: Bias-augmented Consistency Training (BCT), which enforces invariance at the output level, and Activation Consistency Training (ACT), which enforces invariance in internal model activations. Both methods are evaluated on Gemini 2.5 Flash, demonstrating measurable reductions in susceptibility to prompt-based attacks while using the model's own responses as training signal, avoiding the pitfalls of stale training data.
connects: This work extends recent research on adversarial robustness and prompt-based vulnerabilities in large language models. It builds on prior work examining sycophancy—where models uncritically adopt user beliefs—and jailbreaking techniques that exploit models through creative prompting. The consistency training framework itself draws inspiration from invariance-based learning in adversarial robustness literature, adapting these principles to the LLM safety domain. The paper particularly builds on Chua et al. [2025]'s BCT method while introducing the novel ACT variant. This research responds to the broader challenge of aligning models to maintain factuality and safety boundaries despite adversarial prompt manipulations, connecting to the larger effort to make language models more reliable and resistant to user-directed exploits.
enables: This work opens several promising directions for improving language model safety and robustness. By demonstrating that consistency training can reduce model susceptibility to irrelevant cues, it suggests a scalable paradigm for hardening models against future jailbreak variants without requiring constant human annotation or external labeled data. The approach could extend beyond sycophancy and jailbreaks to other forms of prompt-based exploits. Future work might explore more sophisticated prompt augmentation strategies, apply consistency training to other safety-critical behaviors, or combine it with other alignment techniques. The activation-level invariance approach (ACT) particularly opens possibilities for developing interpretable safety interventions by understanding which internal representations drive sycophantic or unsafe behaviors, potentially advancing mechanistic understanding of safety vulnerabilities in language models.
